title: Beef and ale casserole
subtitle: Slow-braised beef chuck in dark ale with root vegetables
excerpt: Beef chuck braised for two and a half hours in dark ale with carrots, celery, onion, and thyme until the meat is completely tender and the sauce is glossy and rich. Made in a single pot. The ale adds bitterness and depth that beef stock alone cannot replicate.
sourceNotes: Beef braised in ale is one of the oldest British cooking traditions, both elements have been available and cheap throughout British history. The French boeuf bourguignon uses wine; the British version uses ale. The result is different: darker, more bitter, and earthier. British dark ales and stouts both work well.

content.text: Beef chuck is the correct cut. It has the collagen that breaks down during the long braise into gelatin, giving the sauce its body. Lean cuts turn dry. Brown the beef in batches and do not rush this step: the colour on the outside of the beef is where the flavour of the finished sauce comes from.

Cook in the oven, not on the hob, for the long braise. The even heat of the oven prevents scorching on the bottom of the pot. A tight-fitting lid is essential to prevent the liquid evaporating too quickly.

Heat the oven to 160°C. Season the {{beef-chuck}} with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper}} and dust lightly with {{plain-flour}}. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a large casserole over high heat. Brown the beef in batches for 3 minutes per side until deeply coloured. Remove and set aside. Add the {{onion}}, {{carrot}}, {{celery}}, and {{garlic}} to the same pan and cook over medium heat for 5 minutes until softened. Stir in the {{tomato-puree}}. Return the beef. Add the {{beer}}, {{stock-beef}}, {{thyme}}, and {{bay-leaves}}. Bring to a simmer, cover tightly, and transfer to the oven. Cook for 2 hours 30 minutes until the beef is completely tender and breaks apart easily. Remove the thyme and bay. Taste and adjust seasoning. Serve with mashed potato or crusty bread.

items.fix: Remove the lid and simmer on the hob for 10 to 15 minutes to reduce. The sauce should coat the back of a spoon
items.cause: Lid not tight enough, or too much liquid added
items.symptom: Sauce very thin and watery after 2.5 hours

items.fix: Chuck needs at least 2.5 hours at 160°C. If still tough, cover and continue for another 30 minutes. Check the oven temperature with a thermometer
items.cause: Cut too lean, or oven temperature too low
items.symptom: Beef still chewy and tough after 2.5 hours

items.fix: A small amount of Worcestershire sauce (1 tablespoon) or a teaspoon of soft brown sugar added at the end can balance excessive bitterness
items.cause: Very bitter ale, not balanced by enough vegetables or sweetness
items.symptom: Bitter, metallic taste from the ale

content.text: Beef and ale casserole is a British winter dish, associated with cold evenings and Sunday afternoons. It reheats perfectly, improves over 2 days, and freezes without loss of quality. It is the kind of dish that is better made in a large batch than a small one, as the effort is the same regardless of the quantity.
